title: MarginalizationsCommute
lean_code_with_comments:
/-- claim_3_17 fusion lemma: iterating two disjoint marginalizations
equals a single marginalization on the union,
`(G.marginalize W₁).marginalize W₂ = G.marginalize (W₁ ∪ W₂)`.
Mirrors the first half of the chained equality in the `\Lem` at
`lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex` line 997
(`marginalizations-commute`). -/
theorem marginalize_marginalize (G : CDMG α) {W₁ W₂ : Set α}
    (h : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    (G.marginalize W₁).marginalize W₂ = G.marginalize (W₁ ∪ W₂) := by
  refine mk_eq_of_data ?_ ?_ ?_ ?_
  · -- J: both `(G.marg W₁).marg W₂.J = G.J` and `G.marg (W₁ ∪ W₂).J = G.J`.
    show ((G.marginalize W₁).marginalize W₂).J = (G.marginalize (W₁ ∪ W₂)).J
    rfl
  · -- V: `(G.V \ W₁) \ W₂ = G.V \ (W₁ ∪ W₂)` via `Set.diff_diff`.
    show ((G.marginalize W₁).marginalize W₂).V = (G.marginalize (W₁ ∪ W₂)).V
    simp only [CDMG.marginalize_V, Set.diff_diff]
  · -- E: walk-existential iff via `directed_walk_iff`.
    ext p
    simp only [CDMG.mem_marginalize_E, CDMG.marginalize_J, CDMG.marginalize_V,
               Set.diff_diff]
    constructor
    · rintro ⟨hp1, hp2, hex⟩
      exact ⟨hp1, hp2, (directed_walk_iff G W₁ W₂ hp1 hp2).mp hex⟩
    · rintro ⟨hp1, hp2, hex⟩
      exact ⟨hp1, hp2, (directed_walk_iff G W₁ W₂ hp1 hp2).mpr hex⟩
  · -- L: bifurcation existential + directed-walk negation clauses.
    -- Structure: unfold both sides via `mem_marginalize_L`; endpoint conditions
    -- match via `Set.diff_diff`; `p.1 ≠ p.2` is shared; the two
    -- `¬ ∃ directed walk` clauses follow from `directed_walk_iff_no_length`;
    -- the bifurcation `∨` requires interior-tracking bifurcation translators
    -- (analogue of `lift_directed_walk` / `shrink_directed_walk` for
    -- bifurcations) -- isolated below in `bifurcation_walk_iff_no_length`.
    ext p
    simp only [CDMG.mem_marginalize_L, CDMG.marginalize_V, Set.diff_diff]
    constructor
    · rintro ⟨hp1, hp2, hne, hnd12, hnd21, hbif⟩
      refine ⟨hp1, hp2, hne, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
      · -- ¬ ∃ directed walk a → b in G: iff version, contradict via LHS.
        intro ⟨σ, hσ_dir, hσ_int⟩
        exact hnd12 ((directed_walk_iff_no_length G W₁ W₂ hp1 hp2 hne).mpr
                        ⟨σ, hσ_dir, hσ_int⟩)
      · -- ¬ ∃ directed walk b → a in G: similar with swapped endpoints.
        intro ⟨σ, hσ_dir, hσ_int⟩
        exact hnd21 ((directed_walk_iff_no_length G W₁ W₂ hp2 hp1 hne.symm).mpr
                        ⟨σ, hσ_dir, hσ_int⟩)
      · -- Bifurcation iff.
        exact (bifurcation_walk_iff_no_length G W₁ W₂ h hp1 hp2 hne).mp hbif
    · rintro ⟨hp1, hp2, hne, hnd12, hnd21, hbif⟩
      refine ⟨hp1, hp2, hne, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
      · intro ⟨π, hπ_dir, hπ_int⟩
        exact hnd12 ((directed_walk_iff_no_length G W₁ W₂ hp1 hp2 hne).mp
                        ⟨π, hπ_dir, hπ_int⟩)
      · intro ⟨π, hπ_dir, hπ_int⟩
        exact hnd21 ((directed_walk_iff_no_length G W₁ W₂ hp2 hp1 hne.symm).mp
                        ⟨π, hπ_dir, hπ_int⟩)
      · exact (bifurcation_walk_iff_no_length G W₁ W₂ h hp1 hp2 hne).mpr hbif

/-- claim_3_17 commute corollary: the order of two disjoint
marginalizations does not matter,
`(G.marginalize W₁).marginalize W₂ = (G.marginalize W₂).marginalize W₁`.
Mirrors the second half (`= (G^{\sm W₂})^{\sm W₁}`) of the chained
equality in the `\Lem` at `lecture-notes/lecture_notes/graphs.tex`
line 997 (`marginalizations-commute`). -/
theorem marginalize_comm (G : CDMG α) {W₁ W₂ : Set α}
    (h : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    (G.marginalize W₁).marginalize W₂
      = (G.marginalize W₂).marginalize W₁ := by
  -- Both sides collapse via `marginalize_marginalize` to `G.marginalize (W₁ ∪ W₂)`
  -- and `G.marginalize (W₂ ∪ W₁)` respectively; `Set.union_comm` identifies them.
  rw [marginalize_marginalize G h, marginalize_marginalize G h.symm,
      Set.union_comm]
lean_code_without_comments:
theorem marginalize_marginalize (G : CDMG α) {W₁ W₂ : Set α}
    (h : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    (G.marginalize W₁).marginalize W₂ = G.marginalize (W₁ ∪ W₂) := by
  refine mk_eq_of_data ?_ ?_ ?_ ?_
  ·
    show ((G.marginalize W₁).marginalize W₂).J = (G.marginalize (W₁ ∪ W₂)).J
    rfl
  ·
    show ((G.marginalize W₁).marginalize W₂).V = (G.marginalize (W₁ ∪ W₂)).V
    simp only [CDMG.marginalize_V, Set.diff_diff]
  ·
    ext p
    simp only [CDMG.mem_marginalize_E, CDMG.marginalize_J, CDMG.marginalize_V,
               Set.diff_diff]
    constructor
    · rintro ⟨hp1, hp2, hex⟩
      exact ⟨hp1, hp2, (directed_walk_iff G W₁ W₂ hp1 hp2).mp hex⟩
    · rintro ⟨hp1, hp2, hex⟩
      exact ⟨hp1, hp2, (directed_walk_iff G W₁ W₂ hp1 hp2).mpr hex⟩
  ·

    ext p
    simp only [CDMG.mem_marginalize_L, CDMG.marginalize_V, Set.diff_diff]
    constructor
    · rintro ⟨hp1, hp2, hne, hnd12, hnd21, hbif⟩
      refine ⟨hp1, hp2, hne, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
      ·
        intro ⟨σ, hσ_dir, hσ_int⟩
        exact hnd12 ((directed_walk_iff_no_length G W₁ W₂ hp1 hp2 hne).mpr
                        ⟨σ, hσ_dir, hσ_int⟩)
      ·
        intro ⟨σ, hσ_dir, hσ_int⟩
        exact hnd21 ((directed_walk_iff_no_length G W₁ W₂ hp2 hp1 hne.symm).mpr
                        ⟨σ, hσ_dir, hσ_int⟩)
      ·
        exact (bifurcation_walk_iff_no_length G W₁ W₂ h hp1 hp2 hne).mp hbif
    · rintro ⟨hp1, hp2, hne, hnd12, hnd21, hbif⟩
      refine ⟨hp1, hp2, hne, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
      · intro ⟨π, hπ_dir, hπ_int⟩
        exact hnd12 ((directed_walk_iff_no_length G W₁ W₂ hp1 hp2 hne).mp
                        ⟨π, hπ_dir, hπ_int⟩)
      · intro ⟨π, hπ_dir, hπ_int⟩
        exact hnd21 ((directed_walk_iff_no_length G W₁ W₂ hp2 hp1 hne.symm).mp
                        ⟨π, hπ_dir, hπ_int⟩)
      · exact (bifurcation_walk_iff_no_length G W₁ W₂ h hp1 hp2 hne).mpr hbif

theorem marginalize_comm (G : CDMG α) {W₁ W₂ : Set α}
    (h : Disjoint W₁ W₂) :
    (G.marginalize W₁).marginalize W₂
      = (G.marginalize W₂).marginalize W₁ := by

  rw [marginalize_marginalize G h, marginalize_marginalize G h.symm,
      Set.union_comm]
lean_explanation:
The two theorems in this file encode the LN's chained equality $(G^{\sm W_1})^{\sm W_2} = (G^{\sm W_2})^{\sm W_1} = G^{\sm (W_1 \cup W_2)}$ as a pair of named lemmas. `marginalize_marginalize` is the *fusion* form, collapsing iterated marginalization onto the union: $(G^{\sm W_1})^{\sm W_2} = G^{\sm (W_1 \cup W_2)}$. `marginalize_comm` is the *commute* corollary $(G^{\sm W_1})^{\sm W_2} = (G^{\sm W_2})^{\sm W_1}$, derived in three rewrites from the fusion lemma.

In the Lean signatures, `α : Type*` is the ambient vertex type, matching the LN's silent assumption that the graph is over some carrier. `G : CDMG α` is the LN's graph $G = (J, V, E, L)$. `W₁ W₂ : Set α` correspond to the LN's $W_1, W_2$, though the LN's $W_i \subseteq V$ precondition is deliberately dropped (see design notes). `G.marginalize W` is the marginalization operation from def_3_15, so `(G.marginalize W₁).marginalize W₂` matches the LN's $(G^{\sm W_1})^{\sm W_2}$ and `G.marginalize (W₁ ∪ W₂)` matches $G^{\sm (W_1 \cup W_2)}$. Both theorems conclude a literal `Eq` of `CDMG α` because marginalization removes vertices but does not relabel the carrier.

The disjointness hypothesis `h : Disjoint W₁ W₂` uses Mathlib's lattice-theoretic `Disjoint` predicate from `Mathlib.Order.Disjoint`. On `Set α` it unfolds to $W_1 \cap W_2 = \emptyset$, but presenting it as `Disjoint` is what lets the commute proof feed `h.symm` (the swapped-argument form, obtained for free from `Disjoint.symm`) into a second fusion call without re-deriving set-theoretic symmetry by hand. `Set.union_comm` identifies $W_1 \cup W_2$ with $W_2 \cup W_1$ to close out the commute proof.

Mechanically, both equalities are proved by CDMG-extensionality on the four data fields `J / V / E / L`; the six prop fields then close by `rfl` because of Lean's proof-irrelevance rule. The `J` and `V` fields collapse by `rfl` and `Set.diff_diff` (using $(V \setminus W_1) \setminus W_2 = V \setminus (W_1 \cup W_2)$). The harder `E` and `L` fields, which use the walk-existential definition of marginalization, are reduced through private walk translators earlier in the file (`lift_directed_walk` and `shrink_directed_walk`, plus their bifurcation analogues) that move walks between the two marginalization views — expanding each marginalized edge to a chunk in the base graph on the way out, and re-contracting on the way back. The commute corollary then assembles in three rewrites: `rw [marginalize_marginalize G h, marginalize_marginalize G h.symm, Set.union_comm]`.
design_choices:
**No `W₁, W₂ ⊆ G.V` precondition — a faithful strengthening of the LN, not a divergence.** The LN states the lemma with $W_1, W_2 \subseteq V$, but in the Lean encoding `G.marginalize W` is well-defined for arbitrary `W : Set α`, so the precondition is unnecessary. Vertices outside `G.V` cannot appear on any walk in `G` — interior vertices of any walk of length $\geq 1$ are forced into `G.J ∪ G.V` by the edge-endpoint constraints, so the predicate `π.InteriorIn W` only ever sees the part of $W$ inside `G.J ∪ G.V`, and none of `marginalize_J / V / E / L` are affected by the "spurious" part of $W$ outside `G.V`. Without this design, the outer call `(G.marginalize W₁).marginalize W₂` would need a hypothesis $W_2 \subseteq (G.\mathrm{marginalize}\,W_1).V = G.V \setminus W_1$, which the LN's informal proof tacitly assumes for *both* marginalization steps without re-justifying it for the inner one. The no-precondition encoding captures that informal usage faithfully.

**Disjointness `Disjoint W₁ W₂` is kept — load-bearing in both the LN statement and the LN proof.** The LN walk-concatenation argument splits a walk through $W_1 \cup W_2$ along intermediate-vertex membership into a $W_1$ contribution and a $W_2$ contribution; without disjointness this split is ambiguous and the inductive identification of "walks with interior in $W_1 \cup W_2$" with the two-step marginalization composition breaks. Mathlib's `Disjoint` from `Mathlib.Order.Disjoint` was chosen over an ad-hoc `W₁ ∩ W₂ = ∅` precisely to get `Disjoint.symm` for free — the commute corollary feeds `h.symm` into the swapped-order fusion call with no manual set-theoretic massaging at the call site.

**Split the LN's chained equality into a fusion lemma plus a commute corollary.** The LN bundles three CDMGs into one displayed equation, but the natural Lean shape is two separately named lemmas. The fusion form is what every downstream consumer actually rewrites with — later induction-on-$|W|$ arguments around bifurcation-preservation and d-separation invariance both collapse iterated marginalizations back to a single $G^{\sm W}$ via fusion, never via the chained pair, and the same pattern recurs in latent-projection / hidden-variable compression, do-calculus iteration, and the discovery pipelines that follow in later chapters. The commute form is the rare-use corollary, needed only when a downstream argument has fixed which $W_i$ comes "first" and wants to reorder without collapsing. Packaging the chained equality as a single conjunction or a transitive `Eq` chain would force every consumer to project (`.1` / `.2` or `.symm.trans`) at the call site — an extra step that obscures the rewrite.

**The LN's third equality $(G^{\sm W_2})^{\sm W_1} = G^{\sm (W_1 \cup W_2)}$ is not promoted to its own theorem.** It is exactly `marginalize_marginalize G h.symm` followed by `Set.union_comm` — derivable in one step from the fusion lemma. Naming it separately would just be the fusion lemma with arguments swapped, bloating the API without adding expressive power.

**Naming `marginalize_comm`.** Standard Mathlib `_comm` suffix for commutativity-of-an-operator lemmas (`add_comm`, `mul_comm`, `Set.union_comm`), pairing naturally with the `marginalize_marginalize` fusion name above.
